enrichment.short_description: d8a is an open-source, warehouse-native web analytics platform compatible with GA4 and Matomo tracking protocols, letting teams own their clickstream data.
enrichment.seo_title: d8a Review: Open-Source GA4 & Matomo Analytics Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: d8a is an open-source, warehouse-native analytics platform compatible with GA4 and Matomo. Self-host it free or use the EU-hosted cloud tier.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
d8a, published at d8a.tech and developed under the GitHub organization d8a-tech, is an open-source, warehouse-native web analytics platform positioned as a data-ownership-first alternative to Google Analytics 4 (GA4) and Matomo. Rather than locking clickstream data inside a vendor-controlled dashboard, d8a ingests events using the same tracking protocols GA4 and Matomo already use, so teams can redirect existing gtag.js or Matomo tracking snippets into d8a with little to no code migration. The project also ships its own native web tracker, currently in beta, for teams that want to start collecting data without depending on either upstream protocol. The core proposition is that analytics data should live inside the customer's own data warehouse rather than a third party's proprietary store, so d8a structures incoming events into a normalized, reporting-ready schema and writes that schema into ClickHouse, Google BigQuery, or flat CSV/Parquet files stored on Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, or a local filesystem. Session stitching is computed on the backend rather than in the browser, which the project frames as more accurate and more resistant to ad blockers and browser tracking-prevention features than client-side session logic.

d8a is released under the permissive MIT license and hosted publicly on GitHub, where the codebase is written primarily in Go (roughly 84 percent) with a TypeScript front end (roughly 14 percent) and Docker-based deployment tooling for local and production installs. Because the license imposes no usage restrictions, organizations can self-host d8a on Kubernetes, bare-metal servers, on-premises data centers, or fully air-gapped networks with no per-seat or per-event licensing fee. For teams that prefer not to operate the ingestion pipeline themselves, the company behind the project (registered in the United Kingdom as D8A TECH UK LTD, incorporated in February 2022 and headquartered in London) also runs a free, EU-hosted cloud version at app.d8a.tech, marketed as GDPR-aligned because of its EU-only data residency and available without a credit card.

Architecturally, the ingestion pipeline accepts hits through the GA4 Measurement Protocol and Matomo's tracking API, validates and enriches each event with device, geo, and referrer parsing, computes sessions and derived fields server-side, and batches writes into whichever destination sink is configured. This flexibility around sinks is one of d8a's clearest differentiators against closed platforms: because the resulting schema is documented and openly available, downstream tools such as dbt, Looker, Metabase, or a team's own SQL dashboards can query the warehouse tables directly, without the row sampling, data-retention windows, or export quotas that constrain GA4's native BigQuery export. d8a also advertises an EU-hosted AI agent layered on top of the warehouse data, intended to let analysts ask natural-language questions instead of hand-writing SQL for routine reporting.

d8a's target market leans toward technically capable teams and organizations operating in regulated or compliance-sensitive industries. The marketing site explicitly calls out healthcare, government, and financial services because of the platform's support for self-hosted, air-gapped, or region-pinned deployments aligned with HIPAA, GDPR, FedRAMP, and SOX-style requirements — categories where GA4's centralized, Google-controlled infrastructure and Matomo's broader all-in-one feature set are less suited to strict data-residency mandates. Because d8a positions itself primarily as an ingestion and warehousing layer rather than a full analytics application, it competes less directly with polished dashboard products and more with other warehouse-native or privacy-first analytics projects such as Snowplow, PostHog, Plausible, and self-hosted Matomo, as well as with the GA4 and Matomo services it is designed to sit alongside or eventually replace.

Pricing is not structured as tiered SaaS subscriptions in the conventional sense. The free EU cloud tier provides a hosted collector and warehouse pipeline at no cost, while the self-hosted, MIT-licensed software can be run indefinitely on a customer's own infrastructure for free. The public marketing pages do not disclose a paid enterprise tier, dedicated support contract, or SLA pricing, so organizations that need contractual guarantees, custom onboarding, or dedicated support would need to contact the team directly; no structured paid plan was found during this research. In practice, the primary cost for self-hosting teams is the underlying warehouse infrastructure (ClickHouse, BigQuery, or object storage) rather than any d8a license fee.

Given its early stage, d8a has limited third-party review coverage — a sparse Capterra listing exists with few reviews — and the project does not publicly disclose team size, funding, or customer counts. It is best suited to engineering-led teams that already operate, or are willing to operate, a data warehouse and that value data portability and protocol compatibility with GA4/Matomo over an out-of-the-box analytics dashboard experience. It is a weaker fit for marketers or small teams that want a polished, zero-infrastructure analytics UI, since much of d8a's value depends on connecting it to a warehouse and a separate BI or visualization layer. Prospective adopters should track the project's GitHub activity and Discord community directly, since features such as the native web tracker are explicitly labeled beta and the product is still actively evolving.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: d8a is an open-source, warehouse-native analytics platform that ingests events using GA4 and Matomo tracking protocols while writing the resulting data into a customer-controlled warehouse such as ClickHouse or BigQuery, rather than a vendor-owned dashboard.

The project is MIT-licensed and available as a free EU-hosted cloud tier or a fully self-hosted deployment, aimed at teams that want data ownership and compliance-friendly infrastructure alongside familiar GA4/Matomo-style tracking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: d8a supports drop-in compatibility with existing GA4 and Matomo tracking snippets, server-side session computation, and export to ClickHouse, BigQuery, or CSV/Parquet on S3, GCS, or local storage, all under a documented reporting-ready schema.

Deployment options range from Kubernetes and on-premises installs to air-gapped networks, supporting compliance-sensitive sectors such as healthcare, government, and financial services, alongside an EU-hosted AI agent for natural-language analysis over the warehouse data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: d8a has no tiered subscription pricing. The self-hosted, MIT-licensed software is free to run on any infrastructure, and the EU-hosted cloud version at app.d8a.tech is offered free of charge with no credit card required.
No public enterprise or paid support tier is listed on the marketing site; teams needing dedicated support or contractual SLAs would need to contact the d8a team directly.

enrichment.short_description: d9 is an open-source, self-hosted fork of Directus 9 that layers auto-generated REST/GraphQL APIs and a no-code admin app on top of any SQL database.
enrichment.seo_title: d9 Review: Open-Source Directus 9 Fork by La Webcapsule
enrichment.seo_meta_description: d9 is a free, GPLv3 open-source fork of Directus 9 maintained by La Webcapsule, adding instant REST/GraphQL APIs to any SQL database.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
d9, documented at d9.webcapsule.io, is an open-source data platform maintained by the French engineering company La Webcapsule as an independent, community-driven fork of Directus 9. The project exists because Directus's core team moved subsequent major versions (Directus 10 and later) to a more commercial, business-source-style licensing model; d9 preserves the last fully open-source Directus 9 codebase under the GPLv3 license and continues to maintain it, explicitly stating it is not affiliated with the Directus core team. The tagline the project uses for itself is that it is 'an Open Data Platform built to democratize the database,' aiming to give technical and non-technical users on the same team equal access to a database's records and digital file assets, regardless of the underlying data model.

Functionally, d9 instantly layers a Node.js-based API on top of any existing or new SQL database. It introspects the target database's schema and generates an abstraction layer without requiring a data migration, meaning teams can point d9 at a production database and get instant REST and GraphQL endpoints without changing how the data itself is stored. Multi-database support spans PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, OracleDB, CockroachDB, MariaDB, and Microsoft SQL Server, making it broadly compatible with the SQL engines most organizations already run. On top of the generated API, d9 ships a JavaScript SDK, a command-line interface for scripting and automation, and a no-code administrative application that lets non-developers browse records, manage digital assets, run CRUD operations, configure user permissions, build complex queries, and automate tasks through webhooks and flow-style automation, all backed by the same underlying API that developers use programmatically.

d9 is self-hosted only; there is no vendor-hosted d9 cloud offering, and the project explicitly frames data ownership and full infrastructure control as central to its value proposition. It can be deployed via Docker, on bare-metal servers, or on any cloud provider a team chooses, which aligns with La Webcapsule's broader positioning around European regulatory compliance, portability, and avoiding vendor lock-in. La Webcapsule itself, registered as a société par actions simplifiée (SAS) in Paris since January 2022 with roughly 20,000 euros in capital, describes its overall mission as building engineering platforms for regulated environments — offering developer self-service catalogs, GitOps-based workflow management, distributed policy enforcement (RBAC, secrets, network policies), and compliance automation across the application lifecycle. Beyond d9, the company also maintains OrbiTS, an MIT-licensed open-source orchestration platform that underpins its commercial engineering-platform offerings, and it sells both a self-managed platform model, where clients own their infrastructure, and an ISO 27001-compliant hosted model with monitoring and scaling included. Individual infrastructure templates are also sold separately, and the company's platforms are listed on cloud marketplaces including AWS Marketplace.

La Webcapsule's disclosed client base spans regulated sectors, including large French enterprises such as Aramis Auto (a CAC 40-adjacent automotive retailer), the healthcare-sector organization OCIRP, the government services group Docaposte, and the energy-sector company Stackease.ai, suggesting the company's broader platform business targets mid-to-large regulated organizations even though d9 itself, as an open-source component, is free for anyone to self-host and use.

Because d9 preserves the Directus 9 feature set and API surface, its primary competitors and comparison points are Directus itself (in its newer, more commercially licensed versions), along with other open-source or headless backend/CMS platforms such as Strapi, Supabase's auto-generated API layer, PostgREST, and Hasura, all of which similarly aim to expose instant APIs over a SQL or Postgres database. d9's differentiator versus these alternatives is its explicit commitment to keeping the exact Directus 9 experience alive under a fully open license indefinitely, for teams that specifically want that codebase's stability and features without following Directus's newer licensing terms.

Pricing for d9 itself is straightforward: it is entirely free and open source under GPLv3, with no paid tiers, seat limits, or feature gates published for the core software. Organizations that want a managed, compliance-hosted version of the broader platform stack (which may incorporate d9 alongside other Webcapsule tooling) would need to engage La Webcapsule commercially for its separate engineering-platform and hosting offerings, which are priced on a custom, contact-sales basis rather than through public self-serve tiers. Given its origin as a licensing-driven fork and its self-hosted-only nature, d9 is best suited to developers, platform teams, and organizations that already value the Directus 9 workflow and want to keep using it without adopting Directus's post-9 licensing terms, or that need full control over where and how their data-access API layer runs for compliance reasons.

enrichment.short_description: Dagster is an open-source data orchestration platform for building, testing, and monitoring data and AI pipelines as declarative, asset-based DAGs.
enrichment.company_size: ~85 employees (as of 2026; now part of Prefect)
enrichment.seo_title: Dagster Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Prefect Merger
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dagster is an open-source data orchestration platform for asset-based pipelines. See pricing, features, and its 2026 acquisition by Prefect.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dagster is an open-source data orchestration platform used to build, test, deploy, and monitor data and machine learning pipelines defined as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). It was created by Nick Schrock, formerly a co-creator of GraphQL at Facebook, who founded the company originally named Elementl in 2018 to build Dagster; the company later rebranded to Dagster Labs. Dagster Labs raised roughly $49 million across a $1.8 million seed round in 2018, a $14 million Series A in November 2021 led by Index Ventures and Sequoia Capital, and a $33 million Series B in May 2023, with additional investors including 8VC, Amplify Partners, Coatue Management, F7 Ventures, and Fathom Capital. The company is headquartered in San Francisco and, as of 2026, employed roughly 83 to 85 people. Nick Schrock has served as founder, and Pete Hunt has served as CEO. In a significant development announced on July 13, 2026, workflow orchestration competitor Prefect agreed to acquire Dagster Labs, uniting the two most widely adopted successors to Apache Airflow into a single company; Prefect stated it will continue to fully maintain and invest in both open-source Dagster and Dagster+, keep pricing unchanged, and that Schrock and Hunt would move into strategic advisor roles while roughly 40 members of the Dagster team joined Prefect.

Architecturally, Dagster's core abstraction is the software-defined asset: rather than defining pipelines purely as a sequence of tasks, Dagster encourages users to declare the data assets (tables, files, ML models, dashboards) a pipeline produces, along with their dependencies, and Dagster computes the DAG of upstream and downstream relationships automatically. This asset-based model, distinct from the more task-centric approach of Apache Airflow or the flow-centric approach of Prefect, is designed to give teams built-in data lineage, freshness tracking, and observability without extra tooling. Dagster pipelines are authored in Python and can integrate with the broader modern data stack, including dbt, Spark, Pandas, Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, and various cloud object stores, and Dagster increasingly markets itself for orchestrating AI and LLM workloads such as retrieval-augmented generation pipelines and agent workflows, alongside traditional ETL/ELT.

The open-source core of Dagster is released under the Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted for free indefinitely, covering the full DAG engine, scheduler, sensors, and local web UI. For teams that want a managed control plane, Dagster Labs sells Dagster+ (formerly Dagster Cloud), a hosted or hybrid offering that adds a managed web UI, catalog search across pipelines and assets, cost and usage tracking, role-based access control, audit logging, and uptime SLAs, while still allowing customer code to execute in the customer's own infrastructure under the hybrid deployment model, or fully in Dagster's cloud under the serverless model. Dagster+ pricing was restructured effective May 1, 2026: the Solo plan, aimed at individual users, costs $120 per month and includes an allocation of 7,500 usage credits, one user, one code location, and one deployment; the Starter plan costs $1,200 per month with 30,000 credits, up to three users, and five code locations; and an Enterprise (formerly Pro) tier is available on a custom, contact-sales basis with unlimited code locations and deployments, dedicated support, and negotiated uptime SLAs. Credits in this system are consumed based on asset materializations and op executions, and additional serverless compute is billed separately if a team uses Dagster's hosted execution rather than the free hybrid model where compute runs in the customer's own cloud account. A 30-day free trial with unlimited users is available for teams evaluating Dagster+ before committing to a paid plan.

Dagster's target users are data engineering and data platform teams, ML/AI engineering teams building agentic or RAG pipelines, and analytics engineering teams that already use dbt and want native lineage and asset tracking layered on top of their transformations. Its most direct competitors are Apache Airflow (the long-standing incumbent it was explicitly designed to improve upon), Prefect (its new corporate sibling following the 2026 acquisition, previously a rival orchestrator with a more Python-native, dynamic-workflow philosophy), and newer entrants such as Mage, Kestra, and cloud-native managed orchestration offered by Databricks Workflows and AWS Step Functions. Dagster differentiates itself primarily through its asset-centric mental model, strong local development experience, and integrated data catalog and lineage features that are opinionated defaults rather than bolt-on add-ons. Reviews on sites such as G2 and Capterra generally cite Dagster's developer experience, testability, and observability as strengths, while some users note a steeper learning curve for the asset abstraction compared to simpler task-based schedulers, and note that Dagster+ credit-based pricing can be harder to predict at scale than flat per-seat pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dagster is an open-source data orchestration platform, created by Nick Schrock and originally built by the company Elementl (later renamed Dagster Labs), founded in 2018 in San Francisco.

It organizes pipelines around software-defined assets rather than plain tasks, giving teams built-in lineage and observability, and in July 2026 Dagster Labs was acquired by orchestration rival Prefect, which committed to keeping both products and their pricing unchanged.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Dagster pipelines are authored in Python and integrate with dbt, Spark, Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, and common cloud storage, with growing support for orchestrating AI/LLM and agentic workflows alongside traditional ETL.

The open-source core (Apache 2.0) includes the DAG engine, scheduler, sensors, and a local web UI, while the managed Dagster+ product adds a hosted control plane, catalog search, cost tracking, RBAC, audit logging, and uptime SLAs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Self-hosting the open-source core is free. Dagster+ Solo costs $120/month with 7,500 credits, and Starter costs $1,200/month with 30,000 credits, both under the pricing structure effective May 1, 2026.
An Enterprise tier is available on a custom quote basis with unlimited code locations and deployments, and a 30-day free trial with unlimited users is offered for teams evaluating Dagster+.

faqs.question: Who created Dagster?

enrichment.seo_title: Dagu Review: Lightweight Open-Source Workflow Engine
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dagu is a self-contained, single-binary workflow engine for defining DAGs in YAML. Free and open source, with optional paid enterprise licenses.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dagu is a lightweight, self-contained workflow orchestration engine positioned as a simpler alternative to Apache Airflow or plain cron for small teams that do not want to operate a full orchestration platform. Its documentation, hosted at docs.dagu.sh, describes the project's philosophy directly: it is 'the workflow engine that doesn't turn into an SRE project.' The core design goal is to eliminate the operational overhead typically associated with orchestrators like Airflow, which usually require a database, a message broker, and multiple long-running services. Dagu instead runs as a single compiled binary written in Go, with no external database dependency by default, storing execution state in local files. This makes it straightforward to install on a laptop, a single server, or a small VM and have a working scheduler, web UI, and execution engine running within minutes.

Workflows in Dagu are defined as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) using a simple, declarative YAML format, with each step able to invoke shell commands, Docker containers, Kubernetes jobs, SQL queries, HTTP requests, or remote commands executed over SSH. The project also supports third-party and official 'Dagu Actions,' reusable action packages maintained in the dagucloud GitHub organization, extending what a workflow step can do without writing custom code. Dagu ships a built-in web UI for visualizing DAG dependencies, tracking execution status, browsing logs, and manually triggering runs, along with standard orchestration features such as parameterization, human-in-the-loop approval steps, automatic retries, cron-based scheduling, and artifact handling between steps. A notable and increasingly emphasized feature is Dagu's built-in Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, which allows AI coding agents such as Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode to author, inspect, and run Dagu workflows directly, positioning the tool as infrastructure for AI-agent-driven automation as well as traditional data and DevOps pipelines.

Architecturally, Dagu supports three deployment modes that let it scale gradually with a team's needs: a standalone mode where one binary runs the web UI, scheduler, and execution runtime together (ideal for individuals or small teams); a headless mode that exposes only the CLI and API without the web interface, suited to automated or embedded use; and a distributed mode with a central coordinator dispatching work to multiple labeled worker nodes, allowing Dagu to scale from a single laptop to a small fleet of machines without switching to a fundamentally different product. The project reports real-world use in ETL and data pipeline automation, legacy script orchestration, media conversion pipelines, infrastructure automation, containerized batch jobs, customer-support automation, IoT device operations, and AI-agent-assisted task execution.

Dagu's open-source community edition is released under the GPLv3 license and is described as 'free forever,' supporting unlimited self-hosted servers and workers along with the full set of core execution and scheduling features, Docker/SSH/HTTP executors, cron scheduling, and up to two API keys. The project's GitHub repository, now maintained under the dagucloud organization (having previously lived under dagu-org), has attracted several thousand GitHub stars, reflecting meaningful adoption within the self-hosted and homelab-adjacent developer community as an Airflow alternative. Alongside the free community edition, the project (through its dagucloud entity) has introduced paid, self-hosted license tiers for teams that need enterprise controls: a Minimum tier at $50 per month covering up to three server licenses with single sign-on, role-based access control, audit logging, and email support; a Pro tier at $150 per month covering up to fifteen server licenses with the same enterprise feature set; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier that adds architecture consulting, custom implementation assistance, and dedicated support. Dagu also offers a managed cloud option, running a hosted server on isolated Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) infrastructure, with a hybrid variant that pairs a managed control plane with private, self-hosted worker nodes for organizations that want to keep execution inside their own network while offloading operational overhead for the control plane itself.

Because reliable public information about the founding date, headquarters, or team size behind Dagu/dagucloud was not found during this research, those details are omitted rather than estimated. Dagu's most direct competitors are other lightweight, self-hosted workflow tools such as Windmill and Kestra, as well as heavier incumbents like Apache Airflow and Prefect, against which Dagu differentiates itself primarily through its zero-dependency, single-binary architecture and minimal operational footprint. It is best suited to small teams, solo developers, and homelab or edge-computing use cases that want DAG-based scheduling and a web UI without standing up a database-backed orchestration platform, while teams needing enterprise governance features can upgrade to Dagu's paid self-hosted licenses or managed cloud offering without migrating off the same YAML-based workflow format.

enrichment.short_description: Daily Stars Explorer is a free, open-source tool that shows the full daily and hourly GitHub star history of any repository, beyond GitHub's 40k-star limit.
enrichment.seo_title: Daily Stars Explorer: Free GitHub Star History Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Daily Stars Explorer is a free, open-source GitHub tool showing full daily and hourly star history, repo comparisons, and activity trends.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Daily Stars Explorer is a free, open-source web tool for exploring the complete star history of any public GitHub repository at a daily and, for more recent activity, hourly resolution. It was created and is maintained by a developer using the GitHub handle emanuelef (Emanuele Fumagalli), who has written about the tool's development on Medium, DEV Community, and ITNEXT. The project is hosted at emanuelef.github.io/daily-stars-explorer and its source code lives in the emanuelef/daily-stars-explorer GitHub repository, released under the permissive MIT license, with roughly 393 GitHub stars of its own at the time of this research.

The core problem Daily Stars Explorer solves is a limitation in GitHub's own star history APIs and in most existing star-history visualization tools: GitHub's public API only returns star events for the most recent roughly 40,000 stargazers of a repository directly, which means popular projects with hundreds of thousands of stars typically show an artificially straight or interpolated line for their early growth on conventional star-history charts. Daily Stars Explorer works around this by fetching star history from both the beginning and the end of a repository's star timeline simultaneously, reconstructing the true daily curve even for very large repositories; the author's own write-ups describe fetching times of roughly three minutes for repositories with 100,000-plus stars on a first request. Once fetched, star history data is cached (with the tool's documentation citing cache windows in the range of seven to ten days across different versions of the product), and users can trigger a refresh to bring the data up to the current day without re-fetching the entire history from scratch.

Beyond raw star counts, the tool surfaces a broader set of GitHub repository analytics: a 'Stars by Hour' chart that breaks down exactly when spikes in popularity happened, using hourly bars layered against the cumulative daily line, which is useful for correlating a star spike with a specific external event such as a Hacker News front-page post, a Reddit thread, a YouTube video, or a viral social media post. The tool explicitly surfaces mentions of a tracked repository from Hacker News, Reddit, YouTube, and GitHub itself, helping maintainers understand what drove a particular growth spike. It also tracks broader repository activity over time beyond stars, including commits, pull requests, issues, and contributor counts, giving a fuller picture of a project's health and momentum rather than stars alone, which are a commonly criticized vanity metric on their own. A side-by-side repository comparison feature lets users evaluate the growth trajectories of two competing open-source projects at once, which is a common use case for developers evaluating alternatives or investors and analysts tracking open-source ecosystem trends. A 'Transform' feature allows different levels of time-series aggregation and can apply Facebook's Prophet forecasting library to visualize trend lines over the raw daily data. Users can bookmark favorite repositories for quick repeat access, export the underlying data as CSV or JSON for their own analysis, and use the interface in a dark mode alongside the default light theme.

Technically, the project's backend is written primarily in Go (roughly a quarter of the codebase), reflecting its role fetching and processing GitHub API data efficiently, while the frontend is built primarily in JavaScript and TypeScript with supporting HTML and CSS. The project is also packaged for local or self-hosted use via Docker, letting technically inclined users run their own instance rather than relying on the hosted GitHub Pages deployment, which is useful for users who want to avoid the shared GitHub API rate limits of the public instance or who want to track private repositories they have access to.

Because Daily Stars Explorer is a free, single-maintainer open-source side project rather than a funded company, there is no pricing tier, subscription, or commercial entity behind it — it is entirely free to use at emanuelef.github.io/daily-stars-explorer, and free to self-host under the MIT license. It has no disclosed company size, headquarters, or funding, since it is not operated as a business. Its closest competitors and comparison points are Star History (star-history.com), a similarly purposed but more limited tool that is one of the most widely used GitHub star-history visualizers, along with GitHub's own native insights and traffic pages, which offer more limited historical depth. Daily Stars Explorer differentiates itself primarily through its ability to reconstruct full daily history past the 40k-star API limitation, its hourly-resolution spike analysis, and its broader activity-metric and comparison tooling, making it most useful for open-source maintainers tracking their own project's growth, developers evaluating or comparing competing open-source libraries, and analysts or content creators researching open-source ecosystem trends for articles, investment research, or due diligence.

enrichment.seo_title: DALL-E 3 in 2026: Status, Pricing, and GPT Image Successor
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DALL-E 3 is OpenAI's AI image generator, now being retired in 2026 in favor of GPT Image models. See pricing, history, and what's replacing it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
DALL-E is the name of a family of text-to-image AI models developed by OpenAI, the artificial intelligence research and deployment company founded in December 2015 by a group that included Sam Altman, Elon Musk, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, and others, and headquartered in San Francisco. The original DALL-E was announced in January 2021 as a research demonstration showing that a GPT-3-style transformer model, trained jointly on text and images, could generate plausible images from arbitrary text captions. DALL-E 2, a substantially higher-fidelity successor using a diffusion-based architecture, launched on April 6, 2022, and was rolled out gradually: a beta period opened to roughly one million waitlisted users starting July 20, 2022, the waitlist was removed entirely on September 28, 2022, and an API for developers followed in early November 2022. DALL-E 3 was released natively inside ChatGPT for ChatGPT Plus and Enterprise subscribers in October 2023, with API and 'Labs' platform access following in early November 2023; DALL-E 3 improved prompt-following accuracy substantially over DALL-E 2 and could render legible text within images far more reliably, and it was built with tighter integration into ChatGPT so users could iterate on prompts conversationally.

As of mid-2026, DALL-E 3 is in the process of being formally retired. OpenAI announced on November 14, 2025 that DALL-E 3 would be removed from the API on May 12, 2026, and Microsoft's Azure OpenAI Service had already retired its DALL-E 3 endpoint on March 4, 2026. Inside ChatGPT itself, OpenAI transitioned default image generation away from DALL-E 3 to its newer GPT Image model line (GPT Image 1, and later GPT Image 1.5) around December 2025, meaning most consumer-facing 'DALL-E' image generation inside ChatGPT today is actually powered by the GPT Image successor models rather than the DALL-E 3 model itself. This makes DALL-E, and specifically DALL-E 3, historically significant as the model that popularized mainstream AI image generation for millions of ChatGPT users, but increasingly a legacy brand as OpenAI consolidates its image generation roadmap under the GPT Image name, which is built on the same underlying multimodal architecture as GPT-4o-class models rather than DALL-E's earlier diffusion-based lineage.

Historically, DALL-E's pricing and access model evolved through several stages: DALL-E 2 initially used a credit-based system for free users before OpenAI simplified to per-image API pricing; DALL-E 3 was made available at no direct per-image cost to ChatGPT Plus ($20/month) and Enterprise subscribers as part of their subscription, with a limited number of free images available to users on ChatGPT's free tier (typically two to three images per day, subject to change), while API access to DALL-E 3 was billed per image, with standard 1024x1024 resolution priced at roughly $0.04 per image and higher-resolution or HD-quality outputs priced between roughly $0.08 and $0.12 per image. As DALL-E 3 sunsets, OpenAI's GPT Image models have become the pricing and product reference point going forward, with GPT Image 1.5 starting at roughly $0.03 per image for standard quality via the API, positioned as OpenAI's forward-looking, 'future-proof' image generation offering.

DALL-E's core differentiators, particularly at its 2022-2023 peak, were tight integration with ChatGPT's conversational interface (letting users refine an image through natural-language follow-up rather than re-writing a prompt from scratch), strong adherence to complex, multi-object prompts, and industry-leading in-image text rendering compared to contemporaries at the time. Its most direct competitors across its lifespan have included Midjourney (widely regarded as offering stronger raw aesthetic quality for artistic outputs), Stability AI's Stable Diffusion and its many fine-tuned variants (notably cheaper and self-hostable), Google's Imagen and Gemini-integrated image generation, Adobe Firefly (with a commercial-safe licensing angle for enterprise creative workflows), and the open-weight Flux model family from Black Forest Labs, which by 2026 was frequently cited as matching or exceeding DALL-E 3's quality at a lower API price point with better text rendering. Typical users of DALL-E have spanned casual ChatGPT users generating images conversationally, marketers and content creators producing quick visual assets, and developers building DALL-E into third-party applications via the API, though the latter group is now being pushed toward GPT Image models given DALL-E 3's scheduled API removal. Given the deprecation timeline, any team or developer still integrating against the DALL-E 3 API should treat it as a legacy integration and plan a migration to GPT Image 1 or GPT Image 1.5 to avoid a service disruption once OpenAI completes the retirement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DALL-E is OpenAI's family of text-to-image AI models, starting with the original DALL-E (announced January 2021), DALL-E 2 (launched April 2022), and DALL-E 3 (released in ChatGPT in October 2023).
As of 2026, DALL-E 3 is being formally retired: OpenAI is removing it from the API on May 12, 2026, and ChatGPT's default image generation has already shifted to the newer GPT Image model line.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: DALL-E 3's main strengths were tight conversational integration with ChatGPT, strong adherence to complex multi-object prompts, and comparatively reliable rendering of legible text inside generated images.

It was accessible through ChatGPT Plus/Enterprise subscriptions, a limited ChatGPT free tier, and a pay-per-image API, though all API access is being phased out in favor of GPT Image 1 and GPT Image 1.5.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: DALL-E 3 image generation was included in ChatGPT Plus ($20/month) and Enterprise plans, with a small daily free-tier allowance, while API pricing ran from about $0.04/image (standard) to $0.08-$0.12/image (HD/higher resolution).
With DALL-E 3's API retirement on May 12, 2026, OpenAI's GPT Image 1.5 (from about $0.03/image) is now the primary supported and forward-looking pricing reference for OpenAI image generation.

enrichment.seo_title: Dashlane Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dashlane is a password manager with AI scam protection, VPN, and dark web monitoring. See 2026 pricing plans for individuals, families, and business.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dashlane is a password management and digital identity security platform founded in 2009 by Emmanuel Schalit, Bernard Liautaud, Alexis Fogel, and Jean Guillou, and headquartered in New York City. The company has raised roughly $192 million in venture funding over multiple rounds, with investors including Sequoia Capital, Hercules Capital, SVB Capital, and SVB Financial Group, positioning it as one of the more heavily capitalized independent players in the consumer and business password management space, alongside competitors such as 1Password, Bitwarden, NordPass, Keeper, and LastPass. As of 2026, Dashlane employs roughly 309 people and reports serving more than 19 million users and over 22,000 businesses globally, spanning both its consumer/personal product line and its business-focused offerings.

At its core, Dashlane stores usernames, passwords, passkeys, payment card details, and other sensitive personal data in an end-to-end encrypted vault, synced across a user's devices via native apps for Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android, plus browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge. The company uses a zero-knowledge encryption architecture, meaning Dashlane's own servers never have access to a user's unencrypted vault data or master password. Beyond core password storage and autofill, Dashlane's feature set includes a built-in VPN for protecting traffic on public Wi-Fi, dark web monitoring that alerts users when their credentials appear in known data breaches, secure and permissioned password sharing, encrypted document storage, and two-factor authentication support. In February 2026, Dashlane launched Passkey Linked Domains, a security enhancement improving how passkeys are matched and autofilled across related domains, reflecting the company's ongoing push toward passwordless authentication as passkey adoption grows across major platforms. Dashlane also markets AI-powered scam protection, which analyzes sites and autofill contexts in real time to warn users before they enter credentials or payment information on a suspected phishing or scam page, an area of increasing investment across the password manager category as AI-generated phishing content has become harder to detect manually.

A significant recent change to Dashlane's business model was the retirement of its free plan: on September 16, 2025, Dashlane formally discontinued the Free tier that had previously let users store a limited number of passwords at no cost. Existing free users were first migrated to a temporary trial of Premium features starting August 5, 2025, and once that trial period ended on September 16, 2025, free-tier accounts lost the ability to add, edit, or view stored data (though users retained the ability to export their vault). Since that change, Dashlane operates exclusively as a paid subscription product, differentiating it from competitors such as Bitwarden and NordPass that continued to offer functional free tiers into 2026, though Dashlane still offers a 30-day money-back guarantee so new subscribers can trial the paid product with a refund safety net rather than an ongoing free tier.

Dashlane's current personal plans include Premium, its flagship individual subscription providing unlimited password and passkey storage, unlimited devices, the built-in VPN, AI-powered scam protection, secure sharing, dark web monitoring, 1GB of encrypted document storage, two-factor authentication, and live chat support, priced starting from roughly the $4-5 per month range when billed annually (promotional first-year pricing has historically been offered to new subscribers, so the effective price can vary by offer). A Friends & Family plan extends Premium-equivalent protection to up to ten family members under one subscription, with the primary plan manager receiving the full Premium feature set (including VPN) while other members receive most features except the VPN. On the business side, Dashlane offers a Starter plan at a flat rate for up to 10 users, a per-seat Business plan, and a more advanced per-seat Omnix tier that adds features such as SSO/SCIM integration, workforce password policy management, and credential breach prevention across domains outside a company's single sign-on perimeter; a fully custom Enterprise tier rounds out the business lineup with dedicated account management and volume-based pricing for large organizations.

Dashlane differentiates itself from rivals primarily through its emphasis on user experience and AI-driven proactive security features (scam detection, dark web monitoring, passkey management) bundled directly into its core subscription, and through its inclusion of a VPN at no extra cost on its Premium tier, whereas some competitors charge separately or bundle VPN only at higher tiers. It is best suited to individuals and families willing to pay for an all-in-one, actively maintained password and identity-security product, and to mid-size and large businesses needing centralized credential governance with SSO/SCIM integration, at the cost of Dashlane no longer offering any perpetually free option as of late 2025 onward, unlike several of its direct competitors.

enrichment.short_description: Dashword is an AI-powered SEO content optimization platform offering content briefs, real-time scoring, and post-publish content monitoring.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dashword is an AI SEO content optimization tool with content briefs, real-time scoring, and monitoring. Plans start at $99/month, free trial available.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dashword is an AI-powered SEO content optimization platform designed to help content marketers, SEO teams, and agencies research, write, and maintain search-optimized content more efficiently. The company was founded in 2019 and is headquartered in Tallinn, Estonia, placing it within a broader wave of Baltic-region SaaS startups; public details about its founders and exact team size are limited, and no material funding history was found during this research, suggesting Dashword operates as a smaller, likely bootstrapped or lightly funded, independent SaaS company relative to venture-backed competitors in the content optimization space.

The platform's stated mission, per its own marketing, is to make researching and writing high-quality content 'easy, fast, and measurable,' and its product is built around three connected workflows. The first is content brief creation: Dashword compiles competitor research, target keyword data, and recommended topics/headers into an interactive brief-building interface, which content strategists can then share with writers so that drafts start from data-backed guidance rather than a blank page. The second is content optimization: as a writer works on a piece (either inside Dashword's own editor or via integrations), the platform calculates a real-time SEO content score that reflects how well the draft aligns with what is currently ranking for the target keyword, factoring in elements like topic coverage, related terms, and structural best practices, so writers can improve a piece before publishing rather than after. The third is content monitoring: once content is live, Dashword continues tracking its search rankings and organic performance over time, flagging underperforming or decaying pages and surfacing weekly keyword reports so teams know which existing articles need to be refreshed or expanded, a workflow increasingly important for SEO teams managing large existing content libraries rather than only producing new posts.

Dashword also includes AI writing assistance, marketed as an AI Writer feature that can help generate draft content within the word allowances of a given plan, positioning the product not just as a scoring or research tool but as part of the broader AI-assisted content production category that has grown rapidly alongside large language model adoption in content marketing teams. Reported customer feedback cited in Dashword's own marketing, including from a co-founder at productivity software company QuizBreaker and quotes attributed to marketing leaders at other companies, describes consistent traffic increases on articles optimized through the platform and simplified writing workflows when strategic keywords are pre-organized in a brief, though such testimonials should be read as vendor-selected endorsements rather than independent, third-party-verified case studies.

Dashword's pricing is structured around two primary named plans plus a custom enterprise tier. The Startup plan is priced at $99 per month and includes roughly 20 to 30 monthly content reports (sources vary slightly on the exact count), 5 user seats, access to the Content Briefs and Content Optimization tools, and an AI Writer allotment cited at roughly 50,000 monthly AI-generated words in some third-party reviews. A mid-tier Business plan, starting around $349 per month, scales this up to roughly 100 content reports, 10 user seats, bulk report creation, API access, and single sign-on (SSO) support, useful for larger content teams and agencies managing many client accounts. An Enterprise tier, cited in third-party reviews as starting from roughly $699 per month, extends further to 100 or more monthly content reports, around 20 user seats, and additional governance and integration features. Dashword also offers a roughly 20 percent discount for customers who choose annual billing over monthly, and provides a free trial (including a full free content report) with no credit card required, letting prospective customers evaluate the Content Brief, Optimization, and Monitoring workflows before subscribing.

Dashword competes most directly with other AI-driven SEO content optimization platforms such as Surfer SEO, Clearscope, MarketMuse, and Frase, all of which similarly combine competitor-driven content briefs, real-time optimization scoring, and (in varying degrees) AI writing assistance. Compared to these competitors, third-party reviews (including from outlets such as TechRadar and Originality.AI) tend to characterize Dashword as offering a strong, near-comprehensive feature set for its price point, while noting specific limitations depending on the reviewer — for instance, some reviews flag gaps or rough edges in particular areas of the product relative to more established competitors like Surfer SEO, which has a larger market presence and integration ecosystem. Dashword is best suited to small-to-mid-size content marketing teams, SEO consultants, and agencies that want an integrated brief-to-monitoring workflow without needing to combine several separate point tools for keyword research, content scoring, and rank tracking.
